data.title: FSMD: A Small- And Mid-Cap ETF With Attractive Valuation, Well-Positioned In The Current Market
data.description:
Fidelity Small-Mid Multifactor ETF (NYSEARCA:FSMD) is among factor-based strategies covering small and mid caps. With a solid track record, attractive valuation, and low volatility, I continue to see this fund as one of the best candidates to outperform once the Fed restarts its easing monetary policy in the coming months, as already highlighted in my previous article covering FSMD in October 2024. And quite frankly, the fund’s recent changes in its portfolio composition, increasing the exposure to small caps, have just added to its upside potential going forward.

ETF Description & Highlights

FSMD offers exposure to small and mid capitalization U.S. companies following a multi-factor approach, tracking the Fidelity Small-Mid Multifactor Index. Starting from a pool comprising the 3,000 largest U.S. companies, but excluding the first 500 names, this index is designed to identify companies with better-than-average valuation, quality, momentum, and low volatility characteristics, following a rules-based methodology. The selected stocks are also equally weighted within each sector and rebalanced twice a year to reflect current market conditions.

As of August 12, 2025, FSMD had $1.7 billion under management invested across 590 holdings, with an average market capitalization of $6.31 billion. Its portfolio tilts heavily toward the small-cap segment (59.8% of assets), followed by mid-caps at 20.6%, and a still meaningful allocation to micro-caps at 18.3%. The major equity index that, in my view, fits the best as a benchmark for FSMD is the S&P MidCap 400, an index where, despite the name, mid caps account for 26.9% and small caps 69.7%. This index has a much higher average market cap, though, driven by its market cap-weighted structure. We can also see that FSMD has increased its exposure to small caps from 54.0% to 59.8% since my previous article covering this fund back in October 2024.

FSMD has a very diversified equity exposure. This is clear if we look at the fund’s top holdings. The largest positions are NRG Energy (0.60%) and Interactive Brokers (0.57%), while the remaining top ten names weigh between 0.40% and 0.55%. These top holdings are also well-balanced across different sectors: EMCOR and Comfort Systems (industrials), Tapestry (consumer discretionary), Live Nation (communication services), AngloGold Ashanti (basic materials), Expand Energy (energy), and Insmed and Natera (healthcare).

The table below introduces a list of ETFs in the small- and mid-cap space that also implement factor-based investment strategies and will serve as the benchmark for FSMD in this analysis. The first is Franklin U.S. Mid Cap Multifactor Index ETF (FLQM), which selects stocks based on metrics like value, quality, momentum, and low-volatility, with a heavy tilt to mid-caps (74%). Invesco S&P MidCap 400 QVM Multi-factor ETF (QVMM) draws from the S&P MidCap 400, identifying companies based on quality, value, and momentum scores, with a solid bias toward small caps. Next, John Hancock Multifactor Mid Cap ETF (JHMM), the largest of the group at roughly $4.2 billion in AUM, favors cheaper and more profitable names primarily within the mid-cap universe, while VanEck Morningstar SMID Moat ETF (SMOT) selects stocks that, according to Morningstar’s view, have competitive advantages and attractive valuations. First Trust SMID Capital Strength ETF (FSCS) screens companies for cash flow, low debt, ROE, and low volatility. Finally, VictoryShares US Small Mid Cap Value Momentum ETF (USVM) applies value and momentum scores, assigning more weight to companies with lower volatility.

FSMD’s largest allocation is to industrials at 19.7% of total equities, followed by financial services at 18.4%, consumer discretionary at 13.9%, technology at 12.5%, and healthcare at 10.8%. Minor exposures include real estate at 6.2%, consumer staples at 4.8%, energy at 4.4%, basic materials at 4.0%, communication services at 3.0%, and utilities at 2.5%. There have been only small changes in the fund’s sector distribution since my previous article last October. Allocations to financial services and industrials increased by 1.% and 1.0%, respectively, while the stance in healthcare dropped 2.2%.

The fund’s sector exposure is roughly in line with the S&P MidCap 400, with a slight overweight allocation to healthcare (+2.5%), communication services (+1.9%), and financial services (+1.4%), while it underweights industrials (-3.4%), technology (-1.9%), and real estate (-1.3%). This gives FSMD a light defensive tilt, reflecting relatively larger positions in healthcare and financial services.

Breaking down the fund’s allocation by sector, positions in industrials are fairly diversified across areas such as construction & engineering, building products, and machinery, with over 10 holdings in each segment and a slight overweight exposure compared to the S&P MidCap 400. FSMD still maintains modest exposures to other segments like aerospace & defense, trading companies, and logistics.

In financial services, regional banks are by far FSMD’s most relevant and overweighted segment, followed by insurance and capital markets. On the other hand, allocations to brokerage and mortgage REITs are quite modest. And in consumer discretionary, the fund largely follows the market capitalization distribution, with broad allocations to hotels, restaurants & leisure, as well as specialty retailers and household durables.

Beyond these three largest sectors, the fund still maintains broad-based exposure across several areas, such as healthcare (biotechnology and medical devices), technology (software), consumer staples (beverage and food retailers), energy (oil & gas exploration and transportation), and basic materials (chemicals and metals & mining).

There are no meaningful sector allocation gaps between FSMD and the peer group average. However, we can see sector-specific positioning playing out for individual ETFs. FLQM and SMOT have over 20% allocated to consumer discretionary, while the peer group average is 15%. In addition, FSCS’s large exposure to financial services (30.4%) contrasts with the peers' average (16.7%). On the other hand, USVM has an unusually high position in healthcare (19.0%), while the peer group averages 11.2%.

As a result, FSMD has an over 85% sector correlation with most funds, with the exception of SMOT (73.4%) and USVM (61.9%), highlighting slightly diversified stock selection strategies. That said, while individual stock overlap is moderate between FSMD and QVMM at around 40%, similar to the S&P MidCap 400, FSMD’s correlation with SMOT and FSCS is pretty weak, below 15%, which suggests some potential for diversification in the small- and mid-cap ETF space.

Slightly Lower Valuation

FSMD’s P/E of 15.6x represents a moderate 9.5% discount to the S&P MidCap’s 17.2x, with nearly all sectors trading at lower multiples relative to the benchmark. Most significant discounts occur in consumer discretionary (-21%) and technology and healthcare (-12%). These are areas where we often see companies trading at high valuation multiples. Thus, FSMD’s discount suggests a disciplined stock selection, as valuation is one of the key factors in the fund’s stock selection process.

On the other hand, the fund’s growth profile is virtually the same as the S&P Midcap 400 average, with only minor sector dispersions such as lower growth in consumer staples and healthcare, but offset by holdings in technology. On profitability, we can basically tell the same story. FSMD’s EBITDA margin of 22.3% is only 1.1 percentage points lower than in the S&P MidCap 400’s 23.4%, while its ROE (18.3%) is marginally higher than the benchmark (16.5%), with only minor sector gaps, driven by FSMD’s very diversified portfolio with nearly 600 holdings and its equally weighted profile.

FSMD trades at a slight discount to most peers, particularly JHMM (P/E 17.4x) and FSCS (P/E 17.2x), driven by FSMD’s underweight allocation to technology (-1.9%) and consumer discretionary (-1,4%), as well as its heavier exposure to small and micro caps, which combined account for nearly 78% of the portfolio. On the other hand, with a 13.2x P/E, USVM is the only fund trading at a lower valuation than FSMD. But this is driven by the same factor that drives FSMD’s valuation down. In the case of USVM, small and micro caps account for around 93% of the fund, which certainly pushes multiples lower, and positions this fund primarily as a small-cap ETF.

Outperforming The Benchmark And Most Peers

FSMD has consistently beaten the S&P MidCap 400 since 2021, resulting in a 5-year 12.9% annualized return that surpasses the benchmark by 1.1%. The fund has also been among the top-performing funds within its peer group, along with FSCS and USVM. While yearly performances have varied, these three funds have outperformed peers and the benchmark over time, despite stronger returns from JHMM and SMOT over the past 12 months.

With betas of 1.04 (3-year) and 0.96 (5-year), FSMD has seen more contained volatility than the benchmark, delivering on its low-volatility mandate. This is underscored by lower capture ratios both to the upside and downside, signaling a trend toward mitigating the average price action seen in the small- and mid-cap category. The fund has been the second-least volatile fund in the group, only behind FLQM. However, it is fair to say that virtually all peers have shown lower volatility than the S&P MidCap 400, as most of them target low volatility as one of their core factors.

FSMD’s average daily volume of $6.66 million is among the largest in the group, though behind peers like JHMM ($11.4 million) and FLQM ($8.01 million). However, its average bid-ask spread (0.24%) is relatively wide and worse than most peers that keep it in the 0.11% - 0.13% range. This arguably makes this fund not suitable for short-term trades. Its turnover of 50% is higher than most established funds like FLQM and JHMM. Despite that, the fund’s expense ratio (0.16%) is far below most peers. FSMD offers a TTM yield of 1.34%, in line with the S&P MidCap 400 and most peers. Finally, its tracking error in the range of 7% to 8% is also similar to peers, while its Sharpe ratio of 0.36 (3-year) and 0.64 (5-year) places it in the first tier, alongside FLQM and FSCS, reflecting a combination of higher returns and relatively low volatility. Sure, Sharpe ratio differences seen here remain relatively small and highlight a high correlation in terms of performance within the group.

Final Thoughts

With a diversified portfolio, FSMD offers broad-based exposure to the small and mid-cap universe, but with a tilt toward smaller companies and defensive sectors. This results in attractive valuations and low volatility without dragging down profitability metrics compared to the benchmark.

While the current environment is still mixed for smaller companies amid slowing economic growth and tariff-related headlines, the medium-term backdrop looks better, as potential monetary easing should work as a catalyst for the small-cap segment in general. This seems like a good setup for FSMD. If current conditions warrant caution, its defensive profile is expected to mitigate the downside risk in the short term. On the other hand, the fund should benefit from lower rates going forward, given its heavy exposure to smaller capitalization companies. Thus, I keep my buy rate on this fund as I see it as a relatively safer bet in anticipation of the Fed's next move.

data.title: It's 'Do Or Die' For Powell: The Jackson Hole Speech Preview
data.description:
The Fed is under political pressure

President Trump wants the Fed to start cutting interest rates and expects the Fed to start cutting interest rates at the next FOMC meeting in September.

Before the July FOMC meeting, President Trump visited the Fed headquarters to "assess the Fed building remodeling" status, and after the visit he stated that the Fed is likely to cut in September.

Trump's main reason for the need for the Fed to start cutting interest rates is related to the US debt. Specifically, the US debt has reached an unsustainable level, where the interest payments on that debt now exceed the defense spending.

The US Treasury has been financing the deficit by relying on more short-term debt, and the short-term yields are directly controlled by the Fed via the Federal Funds rate. Thus, as the Fed lowers the Federal Funds rate, the yields on short-term Treasuries fall, and the interest payments fall. Consequently, the US Treasury could borrow more to boost the economy or finance other Trump programs if the short-term rates fall.

The problem is that the Fed has a clear dual mandate of 1) price stability, currently defined as 2% inflation target, and 2) full employment. The Fed also has a mandate of stable long-term interest rates, which is mainly related to keeping the long-term inflation expectations anchored.

Thus, the Fed's is not responsible for the US Treasury financing issues. In fact, the Fed is statutory an independent government agency specifically due to the fact that it must focus only on the dual mandate, and the Administration and the Treasury cannot interfere with this mandate.

Consequently, Trump's request that the Fed starts lowering interest rates due to the Government finances issues directly interferes with the Fed's independence. In fact, President Trump has been threatening to fire Fed Chair Powell is the Fed does not start lowering interest rates.

Thus, it appears that the Fed is under immense political pressure to lower interest rates in September, and Fed Chair Powell is thus expected to signal the interest rate cut at his Jackson Hole speech.

If the Fed Chair Powell does not signal an imminent cut in the Federal Funds rate, he could get fired. In fact, the Fed Chair can be removed "for a good cause," and the potential mismanagement of the Fed's building renovation could be that reason. Thus, it could really be "do or die" for Powell at the Jackson Hole Symposium.

The Jackson Hole Fed Symposium

The Kansas City Fed hosts an annual Jackson Hole Symposium, which is the major monetary policy platform, usually discussing the most timely topic affecting the global central banks. This year, the topic for the Symposium is "Labor Markets in Transition: Demographics, Productivity, and Macroeconomic Policy."

The Fed Chair is usually the keynote speaker on the second day of the conference, which should be August 22nd this year. The Fed Chairs use this platform to signal the direction for monetary policy, thus it is usually a very important speech, closely followed by the markets.

Last year, Fed Chair Powell signaled an imminent monetary policy normalization after the weak 2024 July labor market report, which was eventually followed by a jumbo 50bp cut at the FOMC meeting in September.

This year, we have a slightly similar situation, as the 2025 July labor market report was also surprisingly weak, considering the major negative revisions to May and June reports. Thus, the market now assignes a 92% probability that the Fed would cut by 25bp in September.

However, other than a weak July labor market report, the situation is now significantly different compared to 2025. Specifically, the current policies in place currently are expected to cause stagflation. Even the Fed is currently expecting slower growth, a weakening labor market, but also rising inflation, based on the June Summary of Economic Projections.

Thus, the Fed understands that there is considerable macro uncertainty, and in this situation an appropriate monetary policy is to wait to assess the net effect of current policies on inflation and growth.

So, how is Fed Chair Powell then expected to signal an interest rate cut in September at the Jackson Hole Symposium, as the market currently expects?

The Powell's speech preview

Powell is expected to acknowledge that the labor market is weakening more than expected, based on the July labor market report. Specifically, Powell is likely to reference weak job creation - but this is not surprising to the Fed. The Fed assessed the current labor market as the "no hire, no fire" market. So, this is just evidence of "no hire".

However, based on the initial claims for unemployment, companies have not started to fire their employees yet. The most recent data shows that the initial claims are still very low based on historical standards and well below the recent levels in June. Thus, this is still a "no fire" market, and Powell is expected to acknowledge this as well.

In addition, Powell is also likely to acknowledge that the labor market is still in balance, based on 1) the labor supply, which has been decreasing, and 2) the labor demand, which has been also decreasing. Thus, even with the increase in the unemployed people, the unemployment rate remained low at the full-employment level, which suggests that the labor market is still balanced. Powell is expected to acknowledge this as well.

With respect to inflation, Powell is expected to say that it's still too early to assess the effects of tariffs and other policies on prices, although the risk is to the upside. Powell is likely to reference a significant increase of the July core PPI by 0.9% MoM, despite the more modest increase in the July core CPI.

At this point, it appears that tariffs are causing higher inflation based on the PPI data, however, the importers are absorbing most of the price pressure for now based on the CPI data. Powell is likely to acknowledge that the risk of an eventual increase in CPI is very high.

Implications

Powell is likely to remain hawkish at the Jackson Hole Symposium. He is likely to assess the labor market as weakening but still in balance. In addition, he is likely to acknowledge that the risk of rising CPI inflation is very high, given the July PPI data.

Thus, Powell is unlikely to signal an imminent interest rate cut in September.

The implications for markets of this scenario are substantial. First, the market expects the first cut in September and two additional cuts in 2025. The S&P500 (SP500) is rising based on these expectations, with PE multiple expansion, while the Shiller PE is already at bubble levels near 40.

If Powell fails to signal an interest rate cut in September, short-term interest rates would increase, the US dollar would rise, and the stock market is likely to sharply fall.

But more importantly, President Trump could proceed with his plans to fire Fed Chair Powell, which would introduce an additional significant risk to the financial markets.

data.title: Tariffs Are Starting To Take A Toll
data.description:
If there was ever any question as to whether tariffs are inflationary or not, yesterday’s Producer Price Index (PPI) report should end the debate. The PPI rose 0.9% in July, which was the largest monthly increase in three years and above expectations for a 0.2% increase. The annualized rate rose from 2.3% to 3.3%. The core PPI rose 0.6%, which was double expectations of 0.3%, and the annualized rate increased from 2.5% to 2.8%. Producer prices lead consumer prices, which means that these increases will eventually show up in some form on store shelves in the months to come. In reaction, the major market indexes gave back the prior day’s gains briefly before at the open before recovering nearly all the losses into the close.

The cost of services rose 1.1% last month, led by a 6.7% surge in machinery and equipment wholesaling, which was the largest increase since March 2022. There were also outsized increases for automotive retailing (4.8%), computer hardware and software retailing (3.5%), among others. Companies are clearly adjusting their prices to help offset the costs of tariffs that now look to be permanent. Prices for goods rose 0.7%, led by a 38% increase for vegetables, while there were also significant increases for coffee, meats, fuel, scrap metal, and eggs.

The fact that the PPI was stronger than the CPI shows us that businesses are absorbing the majority of the increase in costs they pay to producers instead of passing them on to their customers or consumers. This corroborates the analysis done by Goldman Sachs, which I shared last week, indicating that 64% of the tariff costs were being absorbed by US companies through June. Given that the rate of consumer spending growth has slowed demonstrably this year to 0.5% in Q1 and 1.4% in Q2, companies are likely worried about losing market share to cost-conscious consumers. But increases in price through several methods are inevitable if companies want to maintain the profit margins that fuel corporate earnings. When producer prices increase at a faster rate than consumer prices, as we are seeing now, a company’s profit margins get squeezed.

I think there is far more damage being done to small businesses than the publicly traded companies we follow each day. According to the US Chamber of Commerce, there are 236,000 small businesses with fewer than 500 employees that import approximately $900 billion in goods each year. They are the source of more than half our job creation. They don’t have the flexibility or economies of scale that public companies do to absorb tariff costs or navigate around them. These companies are the ones absorbing most of the increase in costs, which may explain the lack of job growth over the past three months. In reaction to these levies, imports are scheduled to decline significantly during the second half of this year.

This is not a good sign for a rebound in the rate of economic growth during the second half of the year, which is the current narrative supporting even higher levels for the S&P 500 by year end. Over the past four months, investors don’t seem to be concerned about the economy at all. There are warning signs everywhere in PMI surveys, labor market data, consumption indicators, but the AI boom has overridden all of those macro concerns to date. I think investors assume that rate cuts from the Federal Reserve staring next month, along with the fiscal stimulus from deregulation efforts, and tax cuts that kick in starting January will lift growth. Perhaps it will, and I have nothing to complain about with respect to year-to-date gains, but risks are building that suggest incrementally building a cash reserve is the prudent move.

Tariffs are taking a big toll on the economy, but they have taken longer and been more incremental than most originally thought, which is why I think investors are complacent now.

data.title: J. M. Smucker: Dividend Aristocrat Navigating Tariffs, Twinkies, And Tight Consumer Budgets
data.description:
Introduction & Financials

The J. M. Smucker Company (NYSE:SJM) is the business behind many iconic American food brands, such as Jif, Folgers, Smucker's, Uncrustables and more, with growth potential coming from recent acquisitions such as Hostess Brands, popular for snacks such as Twinkies. Right now, the stock is trading at some of their lowest levels since 2013, as a result of what they call a “very challenging environment” although the company is taking quick action to address that.

In recent years, the company divested about 30% of their brands, with a significant part of that being pet food, which they sold for a total consideration of $1.2 billion in 2023, the year when they bought Hostess Brands. In recent years, they’ve also been expanding in investing their “largest growth opportunities” such as their Uncrustables brand, which has delivered consistent double-digit sales growth for over a decade and is expected to reach the $1 billion sales milestone soon, especially after their investments in production capacity.

Next fiscal year, the company expects a 2% to 4% increase in net sales, as well as a free cash flow projected at approximately $875 million, as the Hostess brand and their other “key platforms” delivering over 80% of the growth, as well as CAPEX of $325 million and a targeted gross profit margin of 35.5% to 36.0%. As for a long-term target, they say that they aim at achieving $1 billion of annual free cash flows, and I have to say that I personally like it when companies make decisions with the free cash flow in mind, especially when talking about dividend stocks.

For that, lowering the CAPEX also plays an important role, as they were elevated in recent years as a result of their Uncrustables sandwiches capacity expansion. From the looks of it, the company may be transitioning from an investment cycle into more of a debt repayments one, as mentioned below.

Financially, based on their latest report, we see quite a bad position, with the current assets not covering the current liabilities, as well as very significant amounts of debt. The company has announced a plan to continue to prioritize debt repayments by paying down ~$500 million of debt annually over the next 2 years, which would definitely be an improvement, in my opinion. This is important because they basically doubled their debt following the Hostess acquisition, which was not really the best move given the higher for longer interest rates. Due to the environment, there have also been quite significant impairments which hurt the net income significantly, adding to the market’s negative reaction:

We recognized a $867 million impairment charge related to the goodwill of the Sweet Baked Snacks reporting unit and a $113 million impairment charge related to the Hostess brand indefinite-lived trademark.

Not only does it add to the negativity by lowering the net income, but it also makes the market question the purchase of Hostess, which has seen quite an unfortunate timing, as I will mention in the Risks and Opportunities section below.

The company will report earnings on 8/27/2025 (Pre-Market), with a significant drop being expected in EPS ($1.88) and a slight decline in revenue ($2.10 billion), although both are expected to increase later on in the year, especially in Q4, and it’s worth noting that the company managed to beat the annual EPS consensus 13 times over the past 16 years. I believe the key aspects to pay attention to will be their new outlook regarding tariffs and their effect, as well as any potential for faster debt repayments or investments. As I’ll mention below, Brazil and coffee tariffs play very important roles in the company’s, and how they will continue to navigate what they call a “very challenging environment” will be very important for the stock price.

Dividends & Buybacks

Currently, J.M. Smucker is a Dividend Aristocrat, with a nearly 4% forward yield - one of the highest they’ve traded at in recent years on the back of the falling stock price. The company aims at maintaining a 40-45% adjusted EPS payout target, although I prefer looking at the free cash flow. Based on last fiscal year’s free cash flow, the $455.4 million dividend represents a ~55.75% payout, which is not bad at all, allowing the company to increase it further before it becomes an issue. AJM is aiming for a balance between debt repayments, investments, dividends and buybacks, which I think is optimal especially given the current market conditions.

Risks & Opportunities

It’s important to mention that SJM is not the only company in the industry that’s falling, despite overall solid numbers even in this uncertain environment. As mentioned, they have many discretionary products in their portfolio, which people can easily cut on in bad times. A little side note: when working in the pet food retail industry during the pandemic, I’ve seen a significant shift from our premium and ultra-premium products towards the cheaper alternatives, as people either maintained the same budget (while everyone was growing prices) or even cut their spending on pet food. This was actually just the tip of the iceberg, with the trend being visible in pretty much every other industry especially following the post-pandemic wave of inflation, which many companies are still recovering from - think about Nike (NKE), Louis Vuitton (OTCPK:LVMHF) or even PepsiCo (PEP) and most of their competitors, all of which have been reporting issues in consumer demand in recent years.

Another issue that’s tied to their discretionary products are high rates and their effects. By increasing the interest rates, the Fed basically lowers discretionary budgets, forcing people to tighten the belt, which then results in lower inflation, as we’ve seen over the recent years. At the same time, rate cuts can have the opposite effect, increasing these discretionary budgets by lowering the cost of debt of their consumers (i.e. mortgage, credit card costs, etc.). Plus, they can be great for dividend stocks, as the market would have to risk up if it wants to get 4-5% yields that the current nearly risk-free treasuries pay, as well as lowering SJM’s interest payments and making refinancing and investing using debt a much more appealing option.

As for another opportunity, I have to mention the potential for an international expansion. Right now, most of their sales come from the US, with a very small (3.58% of last year’s net sales) coming from Canada, especially after they even divested a condiment business there last year, and ~1.37% of sales coming from other international markets - so, not even 5% of the total sales. This category is so small that they include it in another subcategory, which includes their away from home segment (selling products in restaurants, cafeterias, schools, offices, hotels, etc.), and it could represent an opportunity in the long-term, while the US market could remain the slow cash flow producer.

However, regarding that, tariffs remain a very important near-term uncertainty that recently made them crash, as SJM imports huge amounts of commodities, especially green coffee beans, with coffee being their largest revenue contributor. As Trump said that the US will impose a 50% tariff on imports from Brazil starting in August, SJM is expecting this to be one of their biggest impact drivers, saying that tariffs plus the inflation in coffee prices (that they also help generate) could account for a hit of about $1 to their Adjusted EPS midpoint for FY26, meaning about a 10% hit. I don’t think tariffs are a long-term goal but rather a tool for near-term trade deals, as it’s visible especially following the US-EU deal announced recently. It’s still uncertain, but more clarity in that can result in significant drops in commodity prices (see copper dropping 20% in a day following more clarity on copper tariffs), so the impact may not be that high overall, depending on their timing. Still, SJM is expecting about ~20% net pricing effects (i.e. increasing the prices) as a result, partially offset by ~10% lower volumes expected as a result of coffee’s relatively inelastic demand.

Also, competition ultimately remains a key long-term issue. SJM has strong brands, but so do many of their competitors, and with so many niche products on top, it’s very hard to compete in this market. This is why they have to spend nearly half a billion ($468.2 million last fiscal year) on marketing, which is more than their CAPEX for the year.

Also, the “Make America Healthy Again” policies could hurt SJM as well in the future, as many of their products could even be considered junk food, which this administration seems to be targeting. Plus, the significant growth in GLP-1 (think about Ozempic and Wegovy) use for weight loss could affect SJM especially prior to the 2023 acquisition of Hostess Brands. By reducing cravings especially for the more “discretionary” items like snacks, the timing of this acquisition may end up being very unfortunate. It’s not just that, but after going through this weight loss process, there’s the risk (a risk for SJM and their competitors) that people may be much more mindful about what they put in their body, which can be good for some of the brands (like Jif) and bad especially for the snacks.

However, they are adapting to these changes, recently announcing that they will remove food colorings by the end of 2027 in light of the new policies, and I believe it’s not impossible to see them further evolve while maintaining the strength of their brands. In my opinion, we could be witnessing an accelerated focus on diversification similar to the premiumization that we see in alcoholic beverages companies, with more and more organic, zero-sugar, etc. options for everything they make in the future. For example, Pepsi has recently announced adding a new prebiotic cola to their portfolio, which is just one of many “new” trends in the industry, and this is where brand power and marketing come into play, allowing them to develop new products much faster than a new/unknown brand by including them under their umbrella.

Peer Comparison

Compared to a diversified mix of peers - Kellanova (K), The Hershey Company (HSY), Nestlé (OTCPK:NSRGY), Post Holdings (POST), Flowers Foods (FLO) and Kraft Heinz (KHC) - we see SJM somewhere in the middle-to-low side when it comes to most valuation metrics, which is similar to the stock’s performance compared to these peers.

Similarly, their margins are somewhere in the middle, with overall solid margins compared to peers (excluding the net income, which is negative due to the write-downs mentioned earlier). Especially if they reach their FCF target this year and the $1 billion goal as a result of lower CAPEX and improvements, there is potential to see them deliver better margins going forward.

Valuation

For J.M. Smucker’s DCF valuation, I’ll assume $850 million in free cash flow (2.85% lower than the company expects, to account for uncertainty with a better margin of safety). I will assume a 4% CAGR for the first 5 years (above inflation to account for their FCF growth initiatives), a 3% CAGR for the subsequent 5 years, a 2% terminal growth rate, and a WACC of 6.5% - slightly above my estimate of 6.4% (assumptions: 8.01% cost of equity, 4.97% cost of debt, 21% US corporate tax rate) as well as the market’s for a better margin of safety. This way, we get to an enterprise value of approximately $19.59 billion, and when subtracting the $7.74 billion in net debt, this returns an equity value of roughly $11.85 billion. With 106.68 million shares outstanding, this gives an intrinsic value of ~$111.08 per share. This is almost identical to the current price, so there is arguably little to no margin of safety, unfortunately.

Conclusions

Overall, I believe SJM is a Hold now based on their financials and relatively low potential, as well as no discount to my fair value estimate in a slightly conservative valuation. Their financials aren’t really attractive enough to justify a buy in such a mature industry that could be facing political pressure on top of the current economic one, and the dividend yield also doesn’t seem like enough of a reason alone. Still, it’s on my watchlist, and a potential drop after earnings could perhaps warrant a rating upgrade.

data.title: Silver Market Update - Big Potential Upside
data.description:
By Clive Maund

We got the upside breakout from the Bull Flag that was predicted in the last update posted in early July.

But then, rather like Apollo 12, which got struck by lightning twice in quick succession on the way up, the ride got bumpy for a while, with it reacting back quite sharply to support within the Flag and at the rising 50-day moving average.

But then it stabilized and started higher again last week, as we can see on the latest 6-month chart below.

And it is worth noting that the rather unexpectedly large reaction brought the MACD back to a very modest reading, which means that upside potential has been almost fully restored, and given the overall strongly bullish tenor of this chart, silver has big upside potential from here.

We are somewhat hampered looking at the silver chart in Stockwatch because volume and volume indicators are not available, but we can easily get around this obstacle by looking at the chart for the iShares Silver Trust (NYSEARCA:SLV), which is a reliable proxy for silver itself.

Thus, on the 6-month chart for SLV, we see that the volume pattern is strongly bullish, with big volume on rallies that dies back on the reactions that follow, which is why the Accumulation line shown at the top of the chart is trending strongly higher.

And the fact that it is already making new highs is certainly a propitious indication that points to a strong advance whose first target will be the 2011 all-time highs at $50 that, once cleared, can be expected to lead to rapid upside acceleration.

Zooming out via the 7-year chart, we see that the major bull market in silver is now becoming established and is poised to accelerate, with the price now advancing away from the giant Head-and-Shoulders continuation pattern that formed following the 2020 and 2021 highs.

With the price also being projected higher at an accelerating rate by the boundary of the giant Bowl pattern shown that is shepherding the price ever more rapidly towards a breakout above the resistance approaching and at the 2011 highs at $50.

The next two paragraphs are the same as in the last update because they have the same relevance...

On the long-term chart going back to the start of the millennium, i.e., to the start of 2000, we can see the origins of the resistance that silver is currently working its way through, which is the top that formed in 2011-2012.

Once it breaks clear above the high point of this, which is at $50, it will be in new high ground and free to accelerate away to the upside.

Lastly, it’s worth taking a look at the silver over gold ratio chart for the same time period, from 2000, which shows that despite gold’s big gains from early last year, this ratio is still at a very low level, which means that this sector bull market has much further to go. This sort of low reading means that there remains very little retail interest in the sector - this is very bullish for the sector and for silver in particular, which is regarded as a “steal” at these prices.

The rationale behind interpreting this chart is this: when there is a lot of speculative interest in the PM sector, investors favor silver over gold, because it has the capacity to make bigger percentage gains faster. This is what we saw when the sector peaked in 2011, with silver hitting $50 in the late Spring of that year and gold topping out later in the year in September, which is why the silver over gold ratio hit a peak.

When, on the other hand, the silver over gold ratio is at a low level, it means that speculative interest in the sector is at a low ebb, investors have no interest in it - which is very bullish, as it means that there is the potential for it to go much higher.

This is what we saw at the ratio lows in 2003 before the sector headed much higher, at the lows of the 2008 broad market crash which dragged the PMs down with it, and at the time of the Covid Crash in the Spring of 2020 when we saw a freak low due to the orchestrated mass psychosis that existed at that time.

Finally, an important point to make in closing is that the “window of opportunity” to buy silver and silver-related investments before the big breakout above $50 that is expected to lead to potentially dramatic acceleration is believed to be rapidly closing up, as a breakout above $50 is looking increasingly likely soon.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Applied Materials Earnings: Is The Semiconductor Cycle About To Change?
data.description:
Introduction

The semiconductor market is like the weather: you can enjoy the heat and the sunshine, but sooner or later, you’ll need an umbrella. And if you’ve been following this sector for some time, you know that the “weather” here changes in cycles that are almost impossible to time perfectly. More about the cyclicity, I explained in my previous Micron piece.

Right now, we’re in a sunny cycle. AI data center spending is enormous, orders are strong across the supply chain, and companies like Micron (MU), Nvidia (NVDA), Advanced Micro Devices (AMD), and now Applied Materials, Inc. (NASDAQ:AMAT) are posting strong beats across all of the metrics. But AMAT’s latest earnings call came with a little cloud on the horizon, a reminder that the good times in this industry never last forever, and that the next cycle turn can be coming sooner than we expected it.

Semiconductor Cycle

Semiconductors aren’t just about the companies that design chips. They’re basically the whole supply chain, and the supply chain, as we know, moves in waves.

When the world economy is on the rise, the demand for chips is relentless. Every phone, laptop, electric vehicle, and AI server needs them. In those periods, companies like AMAT, which make the equipment to manufacture chips, can’t deliver machines fast enough. Orders pile up, margins expand, and investors get used to seeing earnings beat after beat. But then something shifts, and this “something” can be many things. It could be a consumer spending that slows, or it could be geopolitical tensions that bring in tariffs. Perhaps customers who made significant purchases in the last two years have a huge inventory, and this is the reason for the weakening demand. I could continue this list for a very long time, but the reason why it’s happening isn’t the primary thing to understand. The crucial part is that here, in this industry, after the uptrend always comes a downtrend. It’s a rule. And suddenly, orders slow down, and margins get squeezed as a result.

The tricky part with semiconductors is that the whole industry doesn’t move in perfect unison, because the industry is huge. As I already said, the semiconductors are everywhere today. You can have one segment, say DRAM, surging because data centers are buying up every high-bandwidth memory chip they can get, while at the same time, chips for phones and laptops might be hitting a slowdown. Foundries could be cutting their equipment orders because smartphone demand is soft, yet AI-related spending is still setting new records every quarter.

That’s why you can’t take one number on earnings of a certain company and based on this decide the whole semiconductor cycle is going up or down. All the different parts of this industry are connected, but they don’t all heat up or cool off at the same time. There is, however, the leading edge (the most powerful chips) that shows the tendency in this market better than anything else (well, at least to my observations). If demand starts slowing there, it could be the first sign that the overall sector might be starting to shift. That’s why AMAT’s Q4 guidance caught my attention.

A Quarter of Records

So what happened this quarter? Applied Materials just delivered earnings that most companies dream about. Fiscal Q3 2025 revenue is at $7.3 billion, up 8% from last year, and non-GAAP EPS reached a record $2.48, up 17% year-over-year. Gross margins were almost 49%, which is exactly in line with this sector, and operating margins were steady at just over 30%.

Every segment contributed to the growth. The biggest driver, as always, was Semiconductor Systems (the tools used to build chips), which brought in $5.43 billion with a 36.4% operating margin. Orders were spread across all of the parts of the production: foundry, logic, DRAM, and NAND, with DRAM especially strong thanks to the AI hardware surge this quarter. Applied Global Services (maintenance, upgrades, and support business for chip-making equipment) extended its incredible streak to 24 consecutive quarters of year-over-year growth. And the Display segment (business that makes equipment for manufacturing screens, like OLED and LCD panels, used in TVs, smartphones, tablets, and other devices), which is the quietest part of AMAT’s business, also showed some momentum with OLED equipment sales picking up.

Cash flow was equally strong: $2.05 billion in free cash flow for the quarter. And AMAT stayed committed to returning capital to shareholders, $1.4 billion went out in dividends and buybacks, and they say they aim to contribute 80-100% of FCF to shareholders over time.

If you just stopped reading here, you would probably think this is the earnings of the prospering company, and that the stock probably surged on the news, but as always, not so fast. Because the guidance sent the stock flying more than 14% after hours, and while I write this during the Pre-Market, the situation doesn’t look better.

The Guidance

For Q4, AMAT is projecting revenue of $6.7 billion at the midpoint, plus or minus $500 million, and EPS of $2.11, plus or minus $0.20. That’s about 5% lower revenue and 9% lower EPS compared to the same quarter last year.

Besides, there is a segment breakdown in the outlook that doesn’t look good as well. For Q4 FY2025, Applied Materials is guiding Semiconductor Systems revenue to about $4.7 billion, which would be roughly 9% lower than last year and about 13% lower than last quarter. Applied Global Services is expected to bring in around $1.6 billion, down about 2% year-over-year but essentially flat from Q3, showing the segment’s usual stability even as customers focus on getting the most out of their existing tools. The Display business is the bright spot in the outlook, with revenue projected at roughly $350 million, up around 15% from last year and 33% from the prior quarter, thanks to a pickup in OLED equipment demand, but it’s the smallest part of the business that won’t be able to offset the slowdown in the Semiconductor Systems.

There are two main reasons for this:

  1. China digestion - AMAT had massive shipments into China in 2023 and 2024, as the management explained during the call. Those customers are now in “digest mode,” meaning they’re using the capacity they already have rather than buying more equipment.
  2. Uneven leading-edge logic demand - Management was expecting a nice, steady ramp in the most advanced chipmaking nodes this year, but Q4 orders are coming in choppy. To me, that’s one of those early hints that the industry could be approaching a turning point. When demand is accelerating, even with full-speed production, these companies can’t make equipment fast enough, and that’s when you see the sector surge. But when that demand suddenly eases, production doesn’t just stop overnight. For a while, you end up with more supply than the market needs. It doesn’t mean it will happen now, but it’s definitely something to look out for in the next two quarters.

On top of that, there’s a backlog of export licenses for China orders that hasn’t been cleared yet, and AMAT didn’t include any of that potential revenue in its Q4 guide. If those licenses get approved, great, but for now, it doesn’t change the picture.

The Policy Wildcard

Less than two weeks before AMAT’s earnings, President Donald Trump announced plans for a 100% tariff on imported semiconductors, with exemptions for companies that build in the U.S. That’s a huge swing factor for the entire industry.

In theory, it could be a tailwind for AMAT, since more onshoring means more demand for U.S.-based chipmaking equipment. But in practice, policy changes like this make customers cautious and can trigger the overall demand to slow down.

In my Micron article last week, I talked about “pull-ins,” where customers order earlier than usual to get ahead of possible tariffs. Micron insisted that’s not what’s driving their upside this year, but we can’t be sure. Applied didn’t say much about pull-ins, but the combination of China digestion, uneven leading-edge demand, and policy uncertainty sounds like an environment where some customers might delay orders just to be safe.

Long-Term Outlook

Management was careful to say the long-term thesis is unchanged. They believe they’re perfectly positioned for the next big shifts in chip technology. They’re also doubling down on U.S. manufacturing, with over $200 million going into a new Arizona facility, on top of $400 million they’ve already spent on domestic infrastructure in the last five years.

So for long-term investors that have at least a couple of years' horizon, AMAT still checks all the boxes with technology leadership, healthy margins, strong cash flow, and shareholder-friendly capital returns. If the cycle changes now, you might find yourself in the red for some time, but many long-term investors find it a good opportunity for dollar-cost averaging.

Conclusion

If you’re here for the long haul, this quarter is just another data point in AMAT’s history of weathering the ups and downs of the cycle. The business is in great shape, and the secular growth drivers like AI, advanced manufacturing, and electrification are fine.

But if you’re trading around the cycle, I am not sure it’s a perfect time to buy the dip. The Q4 guide suggests demand could be getting choppy, and in semis, that’s often how the slowdown starts. It doesn’t mean we’re at the peak today, but it does mean the next couple of quarters could bring the confirmation of a slowdown and a stock downturn as a result.

data.title: Vasta's Higher Price Is Fair Given Market Improvements
data.description:
Vasta Platform (NASDAQ:VSTA) reported 2Q25 results, showing a significant improvement in this scholar-year cycle since I last covered the name near to 3Q24 results.

The operational improvements (growth in students, schools, revenues, and margins) were compounded by an accounting tax de-recognition, which added almost $100 million in (non-cash) earnings.

When removing those effects, the name is still generating much higher earnings for the year, in part justifying the almost 2x in price since I last covered it. At about $365 million (R$1.9 billion), and with a capacity to generate net income of about R$165 million, the name seems fairly valued, but I do not expect the company to continue with these growth rates, given saturation in the market. I maintain a Hold.

2025 Cycle Results

As commented in my original post for the name, the company's results should be read on a school-cycle basis, for which the company provides specific financials (except in Q4). The school cycle starts in Q4 and ends in Q3. That means 2Q25 results are equivalent to Q3 on a cycle basis.

The 2025 cycle has mostly positive news so far.

Growth in underlying operating metrics: The most positive factor is the growth in the underlying operating metrics, with the number of partner schools and students increasing for the first time since 2022. This is an indication of at least a temporary reversal in the Brazilian education industry cycle. The economy and salaries are growing in Brazil, leading to more disposable income and, therefore, more families choosing schools with an education system like Vasta's.

Growth in government contracts: The second leg of growth for the company is offering cheaper systems in bulk for public education, which is handled by the states and municipalities in Brazil. This line is also growing, with approximately R$15 million in new contracts (25% growth). According to the earnings call, the line shows lower revenues on a cycle basis because a large contract (with the state of Pará), recorded all revenues in 4Q23 last cycle (2024 cycle), but only half during 4Q24 (current cycle), with the other half expected in 3Q25 (end of current cycle).

Because of limitations on market size in private education (more below), the public education market is one of the largest potential growth avenues. Still, it will probably continue to represent less than 10% of revenues in the 2025 cycle.

Beware of limitations: I had commented in my original article that most private schools already use an education system, and that the demography of the country is negative for early-stage education. That means the market can only grow as people move from public to private schools, i.e., if the economy improves.

In this respect, we might see a negative leg of the business cycle in the country, as the Central Bank raises rates to cool off the employment market. This might lead to more challenges in education going forward.

For example, when we compare the 2Q25 operating figures with early 2025-cycle (4Q24) figures, we can observe that students of core content fell by almost 100 thousand (7% cycle-to-date). This might be a normal churn potentially.

Revenues and profits improve: With more students and with pricing hikes enacted earlier in the year above inflation (HSD versus MSD inflation, as commented in the 4Q24 call), the company's cycle revenues have also improved, 14% YoY.

This has not resulted in operating or EBITDA margin improvement, with the EBITDA margin cycle-to-date falling 1.6pp. The driver of the fall is, in part, the lack of the full Para contract in the cycle-to-date figures.

Despite this, profitability has improved, with adjusted EBITDA up to R$460 million versus R$430 million last year, cycle-to-date.

Non-recurring factors: The company's IFRS earnings improved significantly CTD and on a TTM basis, showing a profitability of almost $90 million (or around R$550 million). The reason, however, is non-recurring and non-cash. The company changed the recognition of certain depreciable goodwill assets and how they impact cash, triggering a R$532 million add-back in 4Q24 (reflected in FY24 figures and 2025-cycle figures). This affected G&A (add back R$93 million in goodwill amortization), added back R$232 million to tax assets, and reduced R$200 million in interest expenses (on the taxes).

The adjusted EBITDA figures above are already correct for these factors, so they are representative of the actual earnings figures.

Some delevering: Via better profitability (on a recurrent, cash basis) and better management of working capital, the company was able to generate almost twice as much FCF this year as last, about R$225 million CTD. The cash was kept in the balance sheet (invested in securities) and offsets some of the interest the company pays on its debt and the factoring of receivables.

This led to a decrease in net interest expenses, which currently sit at around R$190 million on a yearly basis (versus R$220 million calculated in my previous article).

Higher Valuation is Deserved and Fair

Considering all of the above, the company's net income (adjusted for goodwill amortization) has improved significantly, in this case, driven mostly by the leverage of interest expenses.

With the payment of the second leg of Para's contract in 3Q25, we could potentially see the company posting adjusted EBITDA of R$480/500 million for the complete cycle. If we remove from this R$190 million in interest expenses, R$130 million in CAPEX (PP&E, intangibles, and leases), and R$16 million in SBC, we get to about R$165 million in pre-tax income on a cash basis, which is probably not going to be cash-taxed, given the company's large amounts of NCLs and the preferential tax treatment of books, materials, and education in Brazil.

This is almost twice as much as I had calculated in my previous article, and hence the twice as high price is justified, in my opinion.

Further, if the company keeps those R$165 million in cash income per year on its balance sheet, it will offset R$25 million in interest expenses (at a current interbank rate of 15%). Not a bad addition to earnings.

The company now trades at a market cap of about $365 million, or R$1.8 billion, still a reasonable multiple of earnings (11x).

However, let us consider the risks as well. The economy in Brazil might cool with higher rates, and so the next school cycle might not be as positive as 2025's. This could have an impact on the company's future growth rate, even potentially showing a decrease in revenues and deleverage.

I believe over the long term, the company's conservative growth rates across the cycle are probably closer to MSD, with very little growth in students and some growth in pricing with inflation. At consistent margins, this could be something like MSD growth in earnings, which added to consistent multiples, should lead to MSD yields on the share price (in appreciation).

Further, the company is probably not going to pay dividends or buy back stock, given that leverage is a higher priority. This will add to net income, as mentioned, another 1/2pp of earnings growth.

The aggregate is HSD in growth yield with no cash returns, at least for the time being. This is similar to the earnings multiple today.

That is, the name is fairly valued overall. I do not believe it is particularly cheap considering the market situation, but I understand that others might consider it a buy. I maintain a hold rating.

data.title: SRVR: Strong Data Infrastructure Trends But Poor Fund
data.description:
The economy is becoming increasingly digital, and A.I. / Cloud technologies are fostering important needs for digital infrastructure.

The Pacer Data & Infrastructure Real Estate ETF (NYSEARCA:SRVR) is at the center of this new trend, I give it a hold rating.

The fund benefits from the exposure to a fast growing industry, with positions in data centers and telecom REITs that should see a volume of $1.7 Tn global spending through 2030.

However, the portfolio concentration, valuation metrics and track record are tempering my optimism, preventing me from giving it a buy rating. I think there are better options out there.

The Data Surge

Once a niche, digital infrastructure is now a central area of focus for the global economy: you recall the $1.7 Tn investment volume from the introduction. It is the main driver of the increase of IT spending: +7.9% in 2025, reaching $5.4 Tn, driven by software and data centers.

SRVR is tracking the performance of the Solactive GPR Data & Infrastructure Real Estate Index and holds 24 companies with a significant 75.16% concentration on the top 10. The top 3 alone represent a concentration of 42%.

Looking at the sector breakdown, the fund focuses on real estate (59.90%), communication services (23.82%) and information technology (13.27%).

Key holdings are:

  • Equinix (EQIX), global REIT leader in the data center industry. The demand is strong and the vacancy rates are going down.

  • American Tower (AMT), global network of communication infrastructure like cell towers needed for 5G and edge computing. The business is somewhat stable with long-term lease agreements signed with telecom operators.

  • Digital Realty (DLR), operator of data centers focused on renewable energy (75% of the energy consumption).

As a peer, I will mention the iShares U.S. Digital Infrastructure and Real Estate ETF (IDGT), with a portfolio of 25 companies slightly less concentrated as the top 10 account for 68.63% of the fund. IDGT is focused on communications equipment (31%), digital infrastructure REITs (31%) and semiconductors (11.54%).

I will also mention, with a close positioning, the Global X Data Center & Digital Infrastructure ETF (DTCR). The fund is also significantly focused on its top 10 holdings with a 70.46% concentration for 25 holdings. The fund has a 56% position in real estate, close to that of SRVR and a 40% weight in IT, higher than SRVR with a diluted focus on communication services. The key difference stands in valuation metrics, which we will see below. I previously covered DTCR with a buy rating here, in the introduction when I say that there are better options, I’m thinking about this one.

Financial Metrics

The fund’s expense ratio is above peers, with 0.55%. IDGT stands at 0.39% and DTCR at 0.50%. This higher expense ratio is rationalized by the fact that the fund is bigger, and thus benefits from a larger liquidity: $437.9 Mn of AUM against $117.8 Mn for IDGT and $362.6 Mn for DTCR.

SRVR offers a dividend of 1.18% despite its 30-day SEC yield of 2.31% that suggests little to no problem with its sustainability. The two other peers are aligned with a dividend yield between 1.40% and 1.50%, despite lower 30-day SEC yields for both (1.49% and 1.74%).

In the section above I mentioned valuations, SRVR and IDGT are close with P/E ratios of 43.83 and 41.72 respectively. This is huge compared to the valuation of DTCR with a P/E ratio of 23.99. Let’s look at the performance to see if the higher multiples are justified with higher growth figures.

This is a cold shower for SRVR, as it consistently underperforms peers, and it is not a close match. The fund has not created value over the last 3 years, when peers have delivered double digits growth.

This is the performance as of June, the fund lost an additional 1% in July.

Sector Tailwind

The main driver of the REIT industry now is the increasing use of data linked with A.I. and cloud markets. JP Morgan projects a broad increase of REIT earnings of 3% in 2025 and 6% in 2026, providing a 10% total return. Data centers are contributing to this growth, as for 2025, their earnings are growing by more than 20%. The market for data centers, now worth $270Bn will reach $585 Bn in 2032, exhibiting a CAGR of 11.7%

As the fund is heavily positioned on data centers, with companies such as Equinix and Digital Realty, this is an important source of growth.

SRVR has a criterion of 85% of revenues coming from data/infrastructure activities, ensuring that all holdings are aligned with this trend. This is a differentiating element with peers, as it prevents the fund from holding American depositary receipts like IDGT or large cap companies like Nvidia for DTCR.

Risks

The fund is highly concentrated on top holdings, especially the top 3. This is a risk, as it ties the performance of the top 3 holdings with the performance of the whole fund (they almost account for half of the fund). This also introduces a high degree of company specific risk, like the regulatory scrutiny over AMT’s central role in the economy, which is exposed to monopoly regulations.

Power shortages can represent a risk of operating efficiency. The power consumption is surging now and putting pressure on infrastructure. Gartner estimated that by 2027, 40% of data centers will suffer from the energy availability. This also is linked to the necessity of having efficient power grids. I have an article dedicated to smart grid solutions here, that you might want to look at.

The macroeconomic environment represents a risk for two reasons. The focus on real estate comes with a high dependency on borrowing costs, and the market is now pricing rate cuts by the end of the year, which could, if delayed, have a negative impact on the fund. The other reason is the high P/E ratio of the fund, meaning that interest rates also have an effect on the net present value of portfolio companies, and if rate cuts are delayed, this will affect valuations adversely.

Conclusion

The sector in which SRVR is operating is supported by very strong tailwinds. However, I don’t find the fund to be very convincing after having looked at peers: it is the most expensive one, both in terms of annual expense and in terms of valuations, and this is not justified by the fund’s performance.

I will not rate it as a sell because the underlying industry is very strong, but I definitely don’t recommend buying it when much better options can be found.

data.title: Ulta Beauty Isn't Looking So Beautiful Anymore (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
In late August of last year, I decided to dig into Ulta Beauty, Inc. (NASDAQ:ULTA), the largest beauty retailer in the US. From the locations that it has in operation, it offers up cosmetics, fragrances, skin care products, and hair care products. The business even provides salon services to its customers. At the time, I argued that the business remained strong, exhibiting growth, having excess cash, and buying back shares. On top of this, I viewed the valuation of the company as being attractive. In response to these conditions, I decided to rate it a ‘buy’. And since then, things have gone quite well. Shares are up 51.3%, easily outperforming the 15% rise that the S&P 500 has seen. Today, however, I am making the difficult decision to downgrade the stock. Even though operational performance remains strong and the long term outlook for growth is positive, the business is no longer the bargain that it once was.

Of course, my assessment of the enterprise could change as new data becomes available. And it just so happens that, on August 28th, management will be reporting financial results for the second quarter of the company's 2025 fiscal year. Leading up to that point, analysts expect revenue to increase. But overall profitability is forecasted to decline. That's not terribly surprising considering how net profits have been as of late. Even as the business continues to grow from a revenue standpoint, bottom line results have been mixed, though they have tilted more in the negative direction. If we do see some major change on this front, I could perhaps upgrade it again. But for now, I believe that downgrading it to a ‘hold’ is appropriate.

Not so beautiful anymore

Before we get into the most updated data, which would be the first quarter of the 2025 fiscal year, I do think it would be helpful, considering how much time has lapsed, to look at how the company performed in 2024 compared to 2023. Overall sales increased nicely, climbing from $11.21 billion to $11.30 billion. Even though the company experienced comparable store sales growth of only 0.7% during this time, with the average ticket increase of 1.1% offset to some extent by a 0.4% drop in the number of transactions, the number of stores that it has in operation grew from 1,385 to 1,445.

While the increase in revenue for the company was nice to see, profitability declined, with net income dropping from $1.29 billion to $1.20 billion. Some of this was because of a drop in the firm's gross profit margin from 39.1% to 38.8%. This was primarily because of a contraction in merchandise margins that seems to have been caused by increased promotional activity, as well as other issues like higher supply chain costs and the expenses associated with new store openings. Selling, general, and administrative costs, rose from 24% of sales to 24.9%. This was associated with greater store payroll and benefits costs, and additional strategic investments that the company was making. Other profitability metrics fell as well. Operating cash flow, as an example, dropped from $1.48 billion to $1.34 billion. If we adjust for changes in working capital, we would get a decline from $1.96 billion to $1.79 billion. And finally, EBITDA for the company fell from $1.92 billion to $1.83 billion.

*Average Price & # of Transactions are components of Comparable Store Sales

For the 2025 fiscal year, the only data we currently have involves the first quarter. During that time, revenue came in at $2.85 billion. That represents an increase of 4.5% year over year. A good chunk of this was the result of a rise in comparable store sales of 2.9%, with the company benefiting to the tune of 2.3% from higher prices and to the tune of 0.6% from higher transactions. The company also saw an increase to 1,451 stores compared to the 1,395 stores it had one year earlier.

Even though revenue increased, profitability for the company contracted, with net income falling from $313.1 million to $305.1 million. Some of this was the result of a small drop in gross profit margin from 39.2% to 39.1%, with management attributing that to supply chain fixed costs and store deleveraging, as well as a reduction in other miscellaneous revenue. But the big driver was a rise in selling, general, and administrative costs, from 24.4% of revenue to 24.9%. This was because of deleveraging of store payroll and benefits costs, as well as store expenses. A lot of this seems to be tied to the company's growth plans. Other profitability metrics, however, actually came in strong. Operating cash flow jumped from $159.3 million to $220 million. On an adjusted basis, the increase was more modest from $472.5 million to $484.5 million. And finally, EBITDA for the company expanded from $465.7 million to $473.8 million.

*Average Price & # of Transactions are components of Comparable Store Sales

When it comes to the 2025 fiscal year in its entirety, management anticipates revenue of between $11.5 billion and $11.7 billion. Most of this will come from the addition of 60 net new stores. This, combined with the remodeling of between 40 and 45 stores, can be expected to cost shareholders between $425 million and $500 million in the form of capital expenditures. Of course, that also includes money that is allocated toward other basic capital initiatives. So not all of it is growth-oriented.

Earnings per share should be between $22.65 and $23.20. At the midpoint, that would mean net profits of $1.04 billion. That would be down from the $1.20 billion reported last year. But this does not factor in the $900 million in share buybacks the company is planning for the year, which will reduce the overall share count and implies a reduction in net profits even greater than what my calculation shows. In all likelihood, these figures will mean adjusted operating cash flow of about $1.79 billion and EBITDA of $1.67 billion. If we use these estimates, and we also use the historical results for the company from last year, we can see in the chart below how shares are currently priced. The stock is certainly not expensive. But is no longer as cheap as it was when I looked upon it favorably last year.

There are some other things to take into consideration when deciding whether or not to buy into Ulta Beauty. This includes recent developments like the news that broke on August 14th that Ulta Beauty and Target Corporation (TGT) have decided to not renew their existing relationship that concludes in August of next year. Since launching in 2021, Ulta Beauty has grown to have over 600 shop-in-shop locations throughout Target stores. And the goal was eventually to grow this to 800. It sounds as though Target will continue to sell its products within its stores. But at the end of the day, we don't know exactly what kind of impact this will have on the company and its fundamental performance.

Just as there have been negative developments, there have been positive ones as well. On July 10th, management announced that the company had acquired Space NK Limited, a major British beauty retailer that has 83 stores spread throughout the UK and Ireland, as well as a growing online presence. But unfortunately, we don't know the terms of that arrangement or what impact it will have on the company either.

Hopefully, we will be getting some additional clarity when management announces results for the second quarter of the 2025 fiscal year on August 28th. Analysts currently anticipate revenue of $2.66 billion. That would represent an increase over the $2.55 billion reported one year earlier. However, earnings per share are expected to contract from $5.30 to $4.98, taking overall net profits down from $252.6 million to $226.6 million. In the table below, you can also see other profitability metrics for the second quarter of last year. Even though it would be easy to say that a drop in profits will lead to a drop in cash flows also, that is not a guaranteed thing. In the first quarter of this year, a decline in profitability was met with an increase in cash flows. But I do think investors would be wise to keep an eye on those metrics when the news comes out.

Long term, I will say that I am optimistic about the business. In 2024, the industry was already large, totaling $118 billion in the US alone. That was up materially from the $89 billion it stood at back in 2019. This large market is fueled by 140 million beauty enthusiasts aged 13 and older. What's more, management predicts that the space will grow at a low to mid single digit rate each year through 2027. This certainly offers additional upside for investors in the long run, even though I don't think that upside will be enough to justify further market-beating returns.

Takeaway

Right now, I still believe that the future for Ulta Beauty is bright. But I don't think it necessarily makes for a beautiful prospect. Profit and cash flow issues over the last couple of years have been problematic. The stock is also nowhere near as cheap as it once was. Yes, it does have a massive net cash position of $454.6 million that provides it with significant stability and flexibility. But that's not enough to prevent me from downgrading it from a ‘buy’ to a ‘hold’.
